source.title: Accumulate SSE data lines before JSON decode (tool calling + streaming)
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Fixes JSONDecodeError in Python streaming chat when tool_call payloads contain embedded newlines and are emitted as multiline SSE `data:` entries. Prior code attempted JSON.parse per line. This PR accumulates all `data:` lines for an event, joins per SSE spec, then decodes once. Adds fixtures for tool_call frames and ensures backward compatibility with single-line events.
commits: sdk/python: accumulate SSE data lines per event
tests: add tool_call multiline fixture
docs: note SSE multiline behavior
review_conversation: Reviewer (Maya): Ensure we cap max event size to avoid unbounded memory.
Author (Priya): Added max_event_bytes with a clear error.
Reviewer (Dev): Add link to SSE parsing guidelines.
Author (Priya): Added reference in code comment + docs.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash; released in redwood-sdk-python 1.8.7

source.title: introduce-hedged-retry-hints-for-streaming-clients-crosslang-ergonomic-surface-and-version-bump
source.author: Clara Nguyen
source.release_notes: Added hedged-retry hints to streaming clients across Python/TypeScript/Go. New ergonomic parameter 'hedge_policy' for streaming entrypoints, improved default retry presets for long-tail latency, and synchronized SDK version bumps. Backwards compatible; documented migration examples.
source.description:
Motivation: production inference workloads occasionally suffer long-tail token latencies (cold microbursts, GC pauses, noisy co-tenants). This PR introduces a small, opt-in hedged-retry mechanism and a lightweight stream hint that lets SDKs coordinate a second speculative request when a short latency threshold is exceeded. The goal is to reduce 99.9th percentile token latency for streaming without changing the server-side contract or requiring model changes.

What this PR does (single continuous description): Adds a cross-SDK ergonomic surface for hedged retries and stream hints: Python: StreamingClient.stream(..., hedge_policy=HedgePolicy(...)) TypeScript: streamingClient.stream({ ..., hedgePolicy }) Go: Stream(ctx, opts.WithHedgePolicy(...)) The hedge policy is intentionally configuration-light: backoff_before_hedge, max_hedges, concurrency_penalty_hint. SDKs attach a small opaque hint header (x-rw-stream-hint) so routers/edge can make better scheduling/fallthrough decisions. The implementation keeps token refresh / credential rotation behavior unchanged and integrates with existing retry presets.

Included work: - API surface in each SDK (py/ts/go) with typed docs + examples - Lightweight in-process hedger that duplicates only minimal request metadata for the speculative call; KV cache is reused where possible - New retry presets: hedge-optimistic, hedge-conservative - Integration tests: end-to-end with local serving fixture that simulates 2x latency spikes - Unit tests for credential interplay and cancellation semantics - CI matrix updated to run crosslang hedged e2e on a slow-path fixture - Version bumps: python v3.14.0 -> v3.15.0, typescript v1.28.0 -> v1.29.0, go v1.8.3 -> v1.9.0

Backward compatibility and safety: The hedged path is opt-in and disabled by default in all SDKs. When enabled, the SDK ensures only one response stream is surfaced to consumers: the first to produce tokens. The speculative request is canceled upon token emission and its telemetry is recorded as a hedged_sample. Credentials and session heartbeats are not duplicated; the hedged request reuses the same session token when possible to avoid extra auth roundtrips.

Notes on ergonomics: The API favors simple presets (hedge='optimistic'|'conservative'|'off') and exposes a HedgePolicy object for advanced workloads. We keep streaming iterator/callback shapes identical; hedged behavior is transparent to consumer code unless they opt into hedged metrics.

Security and audit: No new privileges are required. Hedged requests are logged with the same level of audit metadata and sanitized in telemetry.

Related work: ENG-8123 (hedged-retry design), ENG-8199 (router hints), SUP-1402 (customer report of long-tail streaming latency).

Checklist: - [x] API design across py/ts/go - [x] Unit tests for hedger and cancel semantics - [x] E2E slow-path fixture tests - [x] Telemetry hooks and dashboard notes added - [x] Docs and changelog entries - [x] Version bumps and package publish configs updated
commits: c1a7f8d: introduce HedgePolicy type and preserver utilities (python)
b4d2e9a: add hedged-path runner and cancellation hooks (typescript)
f8e3c12: reuse session token for speculative requests and add telemetry labels (go)
9d7b1ab: e2e fixture: slow-path latency simulator and assertions
2a6f4d0: docs: hedged-retries.md and examples/hedge-chat.py
5c1e7ff: bump versions and update changelogs
review_comments: Ethan Park: Can we ensure speculative requests never increment customer billing when they are immediately canceled? Suggest we add a server-side hedged flag so billing logic can prefer fulfilled stream only.

Clara Nguyen: Good catch — we've coordinated with billing (SUP-1402) and added a server hint header (x-rw-hedge-sample) and client telemetry to mark speculative calls. Charging is gated on final token emission; added test to assert no charge on canceled hedges.
Maya Patel: API looks clean, but worry about hidden concurrency on low-memory devices. Please add an explicit per-client concurrency limit and document the default.
Clara Nguyen: Added client-side concurrency_penalty_hint and enforced a default max_hedges=1 per request. Documented behavior in hedged-retries.md.
Jonas Fischer: e2e flaky on windows runner; the fixture timeouts are tight.
source.merge_details: Merged by Maya Patel (squash) on 2026-03-05. Merge commit: squash of 6 commits. Release owners notified and publish pipeline queued.
source.notes: This change is intentionally opt-in. We will monitor hedged_sample rate and 99.9p gains for two weeks before recommending a default preset for high-SLA tenants. Internal rollout plan documented in ENG-8123.
source.egress: Consumers: minimal or no changes. Example migration shown in examples/*.

source.title: credential-stager-and-async-stream-helpers-python-sdk
source.author: Marisol Vega
source.release_notes: Added CredentialStager helper and new async_stream utilities; minor public API additions for session lifecycle. Backwards compatible by default; opt-in via new kwargs. Bump python SDK to v0.24.0.
source.description: Context: Several teams reported friction when integrating token rotation and bursty streaming workloads. This PR introduces a CredentialStager utility (lightweight prefetch + lease queue) and an async_stream helpers module to make backpressure-friendly async iterables easier to compose across long-lived streaming sessions. Motivation: reduce auth-related failed requests during token handoff and improve ergonomic composition for consumer code (less boilerplate around cancellations, reconnection hints, and graceful drains). Design goals: (1) keep public surface minimal and opt-in, (2) reuse existing credential interfaces so cross-SDK parity is achievable, (3) add telemetry hooks for future rollout. What this PR does: - Add redwood_sdk/credentials/stager.py (CredentialStager): prefetches refresh tokens, exposes acquire/lease/release semantics, supports pluggable backoff policies. - Add redwood_sdk/streams/async_helpers.py: provides async_partitioned_iter, drain_with_timeout, and a small stream combinator (zip_async) that preserves cancellation semantics. - Wire CredentialStager into streaming client internals as an optional parameter (client ctor kwarg: credential_stager). - Add docstrings, examples, and migration notes in docs/streaming.md and examples/streaming/credential-stager-example.py. - Tests: unit tests for stager lease behavior, integration test that simulates token expiry during an ongoing stream, and a small e2e that exercises reconnection with pre-warmed credentials. Related: coordinated with TS/Go SDK maintainers to align API shapes (see ENG-1292). Rollout: default behavior unchanged; customers must opt-in to enable stager via new kwarg. Migration: guide included to show how to pass existing credential providers into the stager. Checklist: - [x] Implementation - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration tests - [x] Docs and example - [x] CI green - [x] Cross-SDK API proposal posted
commits: feat(cred): add CredentialStager with lease/prefetch semantics
feat(streams): async_stream helpers (partitioned_iter, drain_with_timeout, zip_async)
test: add unit/integration tests for stager and stream helpers
docs: streaming usage + migration guide
chore: bump pyproject version to 0.24.0
source.small_snippets:
# usage example
from redwood_sdk.credentials import CredentialStager
from redwood_sdk import StreamingClient

stager = CredentialStager(provider=my_provider, prefetch=2, lease_ttl_seconds=30)
client = StreamingClient(api_key=None, credential_stager=stager)
async for chunk in client.stream(...):
    process(chunk)

# partitioned_iter example
async for batch in async_partitioned_iter(source_iter, max_batch=32):
    await handle_batch(batch)
review_comments: Ethan Park: A couple of nits on naming (lease -> hold?) and a question about fairness when multiple streams compete for staged tokens. Please add docstring examples for the fairness behavior.
Marisol Vega: Addressed naming nit; added fairness notes and an optional fifo argument to CredentialStager. Also added an integration test that opens 3 concurrent streams and asserts no starvation.
Sofia Chen: Tests look solid. Can we add a lightweight benchmark to show latency impact when stager is enabled vs disabled?
Marisol Vega: Added scripts/benchmarks/stream-prefetch-small.py and included results in the PR description. Observed median tail-token-latency reduction of ~18% under synthetic load.
Liam O'Connor: One security review item: ensure we never log raw tokens. Audit confirmed no token prints; added a guard in stager to redact potential token reprs.
Security Bot (automated): SAST scan flagged no secrets in code. Note: test fixtures contain mock tokens only.
source.ci_summary: CI: 24 jobs (unit tests, linters, type checks, integration). First run: 2 flaky integration failures (timeouts) on matrix linux-python3.11; retried and green. Coverage: +0.6% on streams package. Lint: one minor typing import issue fixed in follow-up commit.
source.notes_for_release: v0.24.0: Add CredentialStager and async_stream helpers. Opt-in feature to improve token handoff for long-lived streams. See docs/streaming.md for migration and examples.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-1299: propagate CredentialStager shape to TS/Go SDKs and add cross-SDK examples
ENG-1301: add telemetry metrics for stager hits/misses and lease durations
ENG-1302: evaluate queuing strategies under high-concurrency workloads
source.backcompat_rationale: Default behavior unchanged; credential_stager is optional and accepts existing provider objects. Public client surface expanded but existing callsites remain valid.
source.changelog_entry: Added CredentialStager (opt-in) and helper utilities for async streaming; bump to v0.24.0.
source.acknowledgements: Co-authored with Priya Nair (TS SDK) and Jonas Müller (Go SDK) for cross-SDK parity discussions.

source.title: Add offline econ prober and quant preset importer to SDK (local-sim tooling)
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Context: teams frequently iterate on prefill/batching/quant profiles but lack a lightweight way to run unit-econ simulations locally before pushing changes to the platform. This PR adds an offline prober and quant preset importer to the Python SDK so devs can: 1) run fast local simulations against sampled workloads, 2) import/validate quantization YAMLs produced by the model tooling, and 3) generate a compact cost delta report to guide conservative rollout decisions. Motivation: reduce deploy-retry cycles, catch regressions in cost/latency tradeoffs earlier, and make onboarding for non-infra engineers simpler. What changed: added redwood.econ.offline.Prober and redwood.econ.importer. The prober accepts sample sets (JSONL/ndjson) and runs a simulated batching/cache pipeline with configurable SLO and token distributions; it reports token-level unit-econ, cold/cached latency deltas, and a risk score for aggressive quant settings. The importer validates quant preset YAMLs (structure, supported ops, target bitwidths) and emits a normalized preset that matches the platform schema (but does not auto-deploy). Safety: telemetry is opt-in; by default runs in dry-run mode and warns when presets would cause >5% estimated perplexity delta (requires an eval anchor). Backwards compatibility: zero-impact on existing APIs unless users explicitly import and call the new classes. Included docstrings, a short quickstart in README, and a test harness with small synthetic samples. Checklist: - [x] CLI bindings: redwood econ probe/import (shim to SDK) - [x] Importer validation + normalization - [x] Local simulation engine with simple batching model - [x] Unit tests for importer and prober - [x] README quickstart and example sample files - [x] Security review (no network calls by default) - [x] End-to-end smoke run on CI Design notes: the simulator models batching using a configurable window/timeout heuristic and supports per-request token-length distributions. The risk score combines estimated unit-econ delta, latency tail amplification, and a simple quality proxy (if eval anchors provided). Limitations: this is a lightweight estimator and is not a replacement for full tenant-sim runs in the control plane. Migration: none required; consumers can opt into the new API. Example usage: 1) redwood econ probe samples.ndjson --preset local_qs.yaml --slo 50ms 2) from redwood.econ import offline; Prober(...).run("samples.ndjson", preset="local_qs.yaml") Testing & results: synthetic smoke across 3 sample sets shows estimated token cost changes within 8% of platform sim for small models (reconciled by downstream tenant-sim). Shipping notes: add to SDK changelog under "Tooling", callout in release announcement for Platform/SEs.
commits: efb2a4c: add offline prober core and batching simulator
9b12d07: implement quant preset importer and normalizer
a0c9f33: add CLI shim and README quickstart
47d7b51: unit tests and CI workflow adjustments
d29f6a2: fix edge-case for zero-length samples and add defensive validation
review_comments: Ethan Park: Can we keep telemetry off by default and provide an explicit opt-in? Also suggest adding a flag to pin the random seed for reproducible sims.
Maya Chen (author): Telemetry is opt-in; added --telemetry flag and seed option. Updated README with reproducibility note.
Priya Nair: Importer should fail fast on unsupported op types rather than silently downgrading; please add a clear error with remediation steps.
Maya Chen: Adjusted importer to raise ValidationError with suggested remediation and included a mapping table for op fallbacks.
Liam O'Connor: Can tests include at least one realistic sample exported from tenant-sim? We should ensure parity with platform estimates.
Maya Chen: Added a small sanitized sample set derived from tenant-sim runs with matched token histogram; test covers the parity tolerance.
Ethan Park: CI flake on Python 3.9 caused by async test fixture; re-ran and green now.
Triager Bot: security review passed — no outbound connections by default.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash on 2026-02-24 by Maya Chen. Final commit message: `sdk(econ): add offline prober + quant preset importer with CLI quickstart`
source.release_notes: Tooling: Added SDK offline cost prober and quant preset importer. Developers can run local unit-econ simulations and validate quant presets before platform rollout. Telemetry and network calls are opt-in. See docs/sdk/econ-quickstart.md for examples.

Importer validation: rejects unsupported ops and missing fields, normalizes bitwidth targets
CLI smoke: end-to-end probe + report generation on Ubuntu runner
CI: added job econ-smoke which runs a small probe to verify import flow

source.title: ergonomic-session-proxy-and-adaptive-retry-presets-py-ts-go
source.author: Maya Patel
source.description: Motivation: ergonomics around session lifecycle and retry policies across our three SDKs remains a frequent source of customer confusion. This PR introduces a small, language-idiomatic SessionProxy abstraction plus a set of adaptive retry presets that provide sane defaults for streaming and request/response flows. The goal is parity of behavior across Python/TypeScript/Go without forcing a single API shape. Summary of changes: 1) Add SessionProxy (py/ts/go) - a thin ergonomic wrapper over the existing session/credential primitives that exposes: start(), lease(), refresh(), and close() helpers; built-in exponential-with-jitter retry hooks; and a lightweight health-check callback. 2) Adaptive retry presets - preconfigured profiles (interactive, background, batch) that tune backoff windows and idempotency rules. SDKs wire these into their stream managers so clients get better reconnect behavior out of the box. 3) Cross-SDK config normalization - introduce session_config schema with explicit fields (lease_ttl, refresh_margin, retry_profile) and mapping utilities in each SDK to keep platform behavior consistent. 4) Minimal public API renames: SessionManager -> SessionProxy alias (backwards compatible shim) and RetryConfig -> RetryProfile (alias + deprecation warnings). 5) Tests and examples: add end-to-end fixtures that run the same scripted session scenario against local mock server to assert parity of lease refresh timing and retry window alignment. 6) Docs and migration guide: update quickstart snippets and add migration note describing aliasing and recommended upgrade path. Why this is safe: migration shims maintain old names, and defaults are conservative. Breaking changes: none (no removal of existing symbols); we do however add deprecation warnings for the old RetryConfig name to encourage migration. Performance/behavior notes: adaptive retry presets reduce median reconnection time for streaming tears by ~18% in our perf lab for typical interactive profiles (see bench notes below). Checklist: - [x] Implement SessionProxy in Python - [x] Implement SessionProxy in TypeScript and Go with idiomatic signatures - [x] Add adaptive retry presets and mapping utilities - [x] Add unit + integration tests that exercise cross-sdk parity - [x] Update docs / quickstarts / migration guide - [x] Bump patch/minor versions in each SDK package files for release - [x] CI green and e2e smoke verified Bench notes: See perf/benchmarks/session-reconnect/README.md for lab numbers comparing legacy vs adaptive profiles. Migration notes: existing code using SessionManager or RetryConfig continue to work; consumers are encouraged to switch to SessionProxy.start() and to select a RetryProfile (interactive/batch/background) instead of hand-rolling backoff. Implementation details: the SessionProxy implements a small state machine with explicit states (idle, leased, refreshing, closed). Lease renewals are scheduled respecting refresh_margin and are cancellable on close(). RetryProfile maps to a {base_delay, max_delay, max_attempts, jitter_pct, idempotent_safe} tuple internally; streaming reconnection logic consults idempotent_safe to decide whether to resume or fallback to cold-start. Cross-language parity was validated by running the session scenario against a deterministic mock that simulates auth TTL jitter and transient network resets. Related: ENG-7421, PM-328, SUP-9142.

Maya Patel (author): Chose 'Proxy' to convey thin mediation of existing session primitives; added docstring and example to clarify semantics. Open to renaming in a follow-up if patterns diverge.

source.release_notes: Added SessionProxy abstraction with adaptive RetryProfiles (interactive/background/batch). Backwards-compatible aliases for SessionManager and RetryConfig remain supported; RetryConfig is deprecated and will be removed after two releases. New telemetry: session.profile.selected counter. See migration guide in docs/quickstarts/session.md.
source.suggested_migration: Replace SessionManager with SessionProxy (thin alias exists). Prefer using RetryProfile.{interactive,background,batch} instead of custom backoff configs. Example: sp = SessionProxy(config=SessionConfig(retry_profile=RetryProfile.interactive)); sp.start()
source.notes: This PR was intentionally conservative: shims keep existing semantics while enabling a future incremental rollout of stronger ergonomics across languages. Follow-up work proposed: unify telemetry schema for session lifecycle and add server-side hooks to report observed renewal latency.
source.changelog_entry: SDK: session ergonomics - add SessionProxy and adaptive retry presets (py/ts/go); deprecate RetryConfig alias -> RetryProfile; bump SDK versions.

source.title: Docs/examples: add safe streaming retry guidance + sane timeout defaults
source.author: Wei Chen
source.release_notes:
- Docs-only: Added troubleshooting guidance for streaming disconnects/timeouts (retry/backoff, idempotency hints, request-id logging).
- Docs-only: Recommended timeout configuration in examples; clarified defaults and how to override per-request.
- Linked to Known Issues article for deeper workaround details.
source.description: Context: Support is seeing repeat tickets where customers use streaming without explicit timeouts and/or implement unsafe retries (e.g., retrying mid-stream without backoff, or treating disconnects as hard failures). This PR updates the Python SDK docs and examples to make the safe path obvious and copy/paste-friendly, aligned with the escalation/known-issues sprint. What changed: (1) Added a dedicated “Streaming: retries, timeouts, and reconnects” section with concrete patterns: retry only when the stream fails before completion; use exponential backoff with jitter; treat client timeouts separately from server disconnects; log/capture request IDs for Support. (2) Updated examples to show explicit timeout configuration (connect/read) and a recommended baseline for production. (3) Added links to the Known Issues article “Streaming timeouts and reconnect” so Support can reference a single canonical workaround page. (4) Added a small note on idempotency: if the caller may retry, they should provide stable inputs and handle duplicated partial output (client-side) rather than assuming the server will resume. Out of scope: changing SDK runtime behavior or default timeouts in code; this is docs/examples only. References: SUP-1842 (streaming resets, us-east) and SUP-1854 (truncated streams) and Linear ENG-4827. Validation: docs build + example lint; no runtime changes.

examples: configure client timeouts and request-id logging in streaming sample
docs: link known-issues article + add ‘when to escalate’ callout
chore: fix formatting/lint and doc anchors
changed_files: examples/streaming_chat.py (explicit timeouts; retry wrapper; request-id logging)
tests/test_examples_smoke.py (adjusted to reflect new example args)

Priya Shah (Support): Can we make request-id capture more prominent? This is the #1 reason we go back-and-forth. Wei Chen: Updated both examples to print/record x-request-id when available and added a short snippet for customers to paste into tickets. Rafael Mendes (SRE): Timeout guidance should separate connect vs read; also avoid recommending disabling HTTP/2 here (that belongs in KB where it’s caveated/temporary). source.title: Fix SSE keepalive handling and ensure DONE terminates stream
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Context: Support escalations show Python streaming chat can hang when we receive SSE keepalive comment frames and the proxy coalesces lines. Our iterator ignored comment frames but also failed to detect the terminal [DONE] when it arrived without a trailing newline. Changes: (1) tolerate ':' keepalive comments, (2) robustly parse event boundaries, (3) treat [DONE] as terminal even if line ending is missing, (4) add a configurable stream_read_timeout with a clear error message. Includes regression tests using recorded payloads from SUP-4821.

sdk/python: parse DONE without trailing newline
tests: add keepalive + coalesced frame fixture

source.title: Standardize retry/backoff behavior + Retry-After handling (SDK parity v1)
source.author: Sabrina James
source.release_notes: Python SDK now uses standardized retry defaults (429/5xx + network errors) with exponential backoff + jitter and respects Retry-After when present. New knobs: max_retries/max_attempts, backoff tuning, and a switch to disable Retry-After honoring. No API surface redesign; behavioral change is limited to defaults and is configurable.
source.body: Summary: Implements the cross-language retry policy standard for the Python SDK: retry on 429 + transient 5xx + selected network/transport errors, exponential backoff with jitter, and proper Retry-After parsing (seconds and HTTP-date formats). Exposes configuration knobs on the client so customers can tune or disable behavior. Context: This is part of the SDK parity initiative (ENG-5120) to eliminate "works in TS but not in Python" inconsistencies, reduce support cases around intermittent 429/5xx/timeouts, and align SDK behavior with platform signals (Retry-After, request_id). Behavior (defaults): max_attempts=4 total (1 initial + up to 3 retries); retryable HTTP status codes: 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 (and 408 when the server responds with it); non-retryable: all other 4xx (e.g., 400/401/403/404/409/422) to avoid duplicate side-effects and billing confusion; retryable network errors: connect errors, DNS resolution errors, connection resets, TLS handshake errors, and read timeouts raised by the underlying HTTP client; backoff: exponential starting at 0.5s, capped at 8s, using jitter (full jitter by default) to reduce herd behavior; Retry-After: if present on a retryable response (notably 429), we honor it as the minimum delay, clamped to max_backoff to avoid extremely long sleeps in client code. Retry-After parsing details: supports integer seconds ("Retry-After: 5") and HTTP-date (RFC 7231 style). If the date is in the past or unparseable, we fall back to computed backoff. Config surface (new/standardized): ClientOptions: max_attempts (int), max_retries (int alias; deprecated warning if both set and disagree), initial_backoff (seconds), max_backoff (seconds), jitter ("full" | "none"), retry_on_status (list override), respect_retry_after (bool, default true). Request-level override: per-request options allow disabling retries (retries=0) for non-idempotent calls or workflows where the caller already handles retries. Notes on streaming: this PR does NOT add automatic retries for streaming responses. Streaming retries remain opt-in and should be implemented at the application layer with idempotency guidance (tracked separately); the retry middleware explicitly bypasses streaming iterators to avoid duplicating partial outputs. Implementation overview: Added a RetryPolicy abstraction used by both sync and async clients; implemented RetryAfterParser that accepts seconds or HTTP-date and returns a delay; integrated policy into the HTTP transport wrapper to centralize decision logic (status/network classification, sleep, attempt counting); ensured we propagate request_id into raised errors (when present) so callers can correlate retries with server logs; added unit tests using mocked responses and transport exceptions; updated docstrings and examples for configuration knobs and safe defaults. Files/areas changed (high level): redwood/_http/retry.py (new RetryPolicy + backoff), redwood/_http/transport.py (wiring), redwood/_client.py (options plumbed), redwood/errors.py (ensure request_id survives wrapping), tests/test_retry_policy.py, tests/test_retry_after.py, tests/test_transport_retries.py, docsstrings in client options and README snippets. Illustrative snippet (Python): options = RedwoodOptions(api_key=..., max_attempts=6, initial_backoff=0.25, max_backoff=10.0, respect_retry_after=True); client = Redwood(options=options) // client.chat.completions.create(...). Alternatively per-request: client.chat.completions.create(..., request_options={"max_attempts": 1}) to disable retries. Why these defaults: SRE guidance was to avoid retry storms under elevated 429s; honoring Retry-After and adding jitter is critical for protecting the gateway and improving tail latency. We intentionally do not retry 400-series validation errors to avoid duplicate actions and billing disputes (see SUP-29438). Testing: Unit tests cover: 429 with Retry-After seconds; 429 with Retry-After HTTP-date; malformed Retry-After; Retry-After in the past; 500/502/503/504 backoff increments and jitter; non-retryable 400 does not retry; transport exceptions (connect/reset/read timeout) retry; max_attempts respected; sleep is not invoked when retries disabled; streaming path bypasses retry wrapper. Rollout/compat: This changes default behavior for some callers who previously observed fewer retries (or immediate retries). Knobs are exposed; we also log a warning if legacy option names are used. Docs/migration notes will be updated in a separate docs PR for the cross-language parity release. Review notes: Ibrahim requested we clamp Retry-After to max_backoff (done) and ensure HTTP-date parsing uses timezone-aware values (done). Allison requested full-jitter by default and to document attempt counts (done). Marcus requested that we never log api_key values and that error messages mention configuration without printing secrets (no secrets logged; confirmed). Review conversation (highlights): Monica Patel: "Can we ensure Retry-After date parsing handles proxies that send RFC1123 with GMT?" Sabrina: "Yes, using email.utils.parsedate_to_datetime + UTC normalization; added tests." Ibrahim Khan: "Please avoid retrying 409s; TS had a bug retrying 400/409 and it caused duplicate side-effect concerns." Sabrina: "Confirmed: only 408/429 and 5xx per standard; added explicit test for 409." Allison Grant: "Default max_attempts=4 (3 retries) seems right; add jitter to reduce synchronization." Sabrina: "Implemented full-jitter; added note in docstring." CI: Initial run failed on lint (unused import in retry.py) and a flaky timing assertion in one test; fixed by mocking sleep and asserting call count/delays deterministically. Final checks: python-unit-tests (pass), lint (pass), typecheck (pass), packaging (pass). Merge: Squash merged into main; follow-up tickets: add conformance fixtures runner integration (ENG-5127) and update docs/migration notes (ENG-5128).
commits: sdk/python: add RetryPolicy + BackoffStrategy w/ jitter
sdk/python: parse and honor Retry-After (seconds + HTTP-date)
sdk/python: wire retry policy into sync + async transports
tests: add retry policy coverage for status codes + transport errors
docsstrings: document retry knobs + defaults
requested_changes: Clamp Retry-After-derived delay to max_backoff; handle past-date headers; add explicit non-retry tests for 409/400; avoid sleeping in tests (mock sleep).

text: Summary: Implements the cross-language retry policy standard for the Python SDK: retry on 429 + transient 5xx + selected network/transport errors, exponential backoff with jitter, and proper Retry-After parsing (seconds and HTTP-date formats). Exposes configuration knobs on the client so customers can tune or disable behavior. Context: This is part of the SDK parity initiative (ENG-5120) to eliminate "works in TS but not in Python" inconsistencies, reduce support cases around intermittent 429/5xx/timeouts, and align SDK behavior with platform signals (Retry-After, request_id). Behavior (defaults): max_attempts=4 total (1 initial + up to 3 retries); retryable HTTP status codes: 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 (and 408 when the server responds with it); non-retryable: all other 4xx (e.g., 400/401/403/404/409/422) to avoid duplicate side-effects and billing confusion; retryable network errors: connect errors, DNS resolution errors, connection resets, TLS handshake errors, and read timeouts raised by the underlying HTTP client; backoff: exponential starting at 0.5s, capped at 8s, using jitter (full jitter by default) to reduce herd behavior; Retry-After: if present on a retryable response (notably 429), we honor it as the minimum delay, clamped to max_backoff to avoid extremely long sleeps in client code. Retry-After parsing details: supports integer seconds ("Retry-After: 5") and HTTP-date (RFC 7231 style). If the date is in the past or unparseable, we fall back to computed backoff. Config surface (new/standardized): ClientOptions: max_attempts (int), max_retries (int alias; deprecated warning if both set and disagree), initial_backoff (seconds), max_backoff (seconds), jitter ("full" | "none"), retry_on_status (list override), respect_retry_after (bool, default true). Request-level override: per-request options allow disabling retries (retries=0) for non-idempotent calls or workflows where the caller already handles retries. Notes on streaming: this PR does NOT add automatic retries for streaming responses. Streaming retries remain opt-in and should be implemented at the application layer with idempotency guidance (tracked separately); the retry middleware explicitly bypasses streaming iterators to avoid duplicating partial outputs. Implementation overview: Added a RetryPolicy abstraction used by both sync and async clients; implemented RetryAfterParser that accepts seconds or HTTP-date and returns a delay; integrated policy into the HTTP transport wrapper to centralize decision logic (status/network classification, sleep, attempt counting); ensured we propagate request_id into raised errors (when present) so callers can correlate retries with server logs; added unit tests using mocked responses and transport exceptions; updated docstrings and examples for configuration knobs and safe defaults. Files/areas changed (high level): redwood/_http/retry.py (new RetryPolicy + backoff), redwood/_http/transport.py (wiring), redwood/_client.py (options plumbed), redwood/errors.py (ensure request_id survives wrapping), tests/test_retry_policy.py, tests/test_retry_after.py, tests/test_transport_retries.py, docsstrings in client options and README snippets. Illustrative snippet (Python): options = RedwoodOptions(api_key=..., max_attempts=6, initial_backoff=0.25, max_backoff=10.0, respect_retry_after=True); client = Redwood(options=options) // client.chat.completions.create(...). Alternatively per-request: client.chat.completions.create(..., request_options={"max_attempts": 1}) to disable retries. Why these defaults: SRE guidance was to avoid retry storms under elevated 429s; honoring Retry-After and adding jitter is critical for protecting the gateway and improving tail latency. We intentionally do not retry 400-series validation errors to avoid duplicate actions and billing disputes (see SUP-29438). Testing: Unit tests cover: 429 with Retry-After seconds; 429 with Retry-After HTTP-date; malformed Retry-After; Retry-After in the past; 500/502/503/504 backoff increments and jitter; non-retryable 400 does not retry; transport exceptions (connect/reset/read timeout) retry; max_attempts respected; sleep is not invoked when retries disabled; streaming path bypasses retry wrapper. Rollout/compat: This changes default behavior for some callers who previously observed fewer retries (or immediate retries). Knobs are exposed; we also log a warning if legacy option names are used. source.title: sdk: add helpers to query active optimization config version + pin version for deployments
source.author: Felix Schneider
source.body:
## Summary
This adds first-class Python SDK helpers for the Safe Apply + Rollback project so operators and customer admins can (1) query which Optimization Config version is currently active for a given deployment/scope and (2) pin a deployment to a specific config version (with optional validation-only mode).

These helpers wrap the control plane APIs introduced in the platform work (Optimization Config Service) and standardize:
- request/response models (including scope fields)
- error handling for common authz/RBAC failures
- safe defaults (read-only methods require no additional permissions; pin requires explicit role)

## Motivation
Support and on-call workflows need a consistent way to answer "what optimization config is active right now?" and to execute a controlled rollback/pin without spelunking through raw endpoints. This is also needed for Private / Dedicated automation (Terraform/installer aside) where Python scripts are used to coordinate changes.

## API (new)
### Client helpers
- `client.optimization_configs.get_active_version(...)`
  - Returns the active config version for a given `deployment_id` and optional scope filters.
  - Intended for debugging, audit correlation, and post-change verification.

- `client.optimization_configs.pin_version(...)`
  - Pins a deployment to a specific `config_version_id`.
  - Supports `dry_run=True` to validate permissions and scope compatibility without applying.
  - Returns an operation payload (request id, effective scope, and the pinned version).

### Data models
- `OptimizationConfigScope` (tenant/model/region)
- `OptimizationConfigVersionSummary` (id, created_at, created_by, state)
- `OptimizationConfigPinResult` (deployment_id, pinned_version_id, effective_scope, request_id)

## Notes on permissions / RBAC
- `get_active_version` surfaces permission errors as `RedwoodPermissionError` with the server-provided `required_permission` when present.
- `pin_version` requires the new "optimization change operator" permission on the target scope. The SDK adds a short troubleshooting hint in the exception message (e.g., "Ask an org admin to grant optimization_config:apply").

## Implementation details
- Adds `OptimizationConfigsAPI` module under the client with clear docstrings and type hints.
- Uses existing request plumbing (no new HTTP stack).
- Both helpers accept `idempotency_key` and forward it to the API via `Idempotency-Key` header.
- Normalizes config version identifiers to string and validates non-empty.

## Example
```py
from redwood import Redwood

rw = Redwood(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"])

active = rw.optimization_configs.get_active_version(
    deployment_id="dep_7f8c...",
    scope={"model": "llama-3.1-70b", "region": "us-east-1"},
)
print(active.id)

# Pin (used for rollback/pinning during incident response)
rw.optimization_configs.pin_version(
    deployment_id="dep_7f8c...",
    config_version_id="ocv_20260122_0012",
    scope={"model": "llama-3.1-70b", "region": "us-east-1"},
    idempotency_key="rollback-2026-01-22-sev2",
)
```

## Testing
- Unit tests added for:
  - happy path parsing for `get_active_version`
  - pin request shape + idempotency header propagation
  - RBAC denied responses (403) -> `RedwoodPermissionError`
  - invalid inputs (empty deployment_id/version_id)
- Tests use `responses` to mock control plane endpoints.

## Backwards compatibility
No breaking changes. New methods only. No existing names changed.

## Release notes
- Python SDK: add Optimization Config version helpers (`get_active_version`, `pin_version`) for Safe Apply/Rollback workflows.

---
### PR checklist
- [x] Added unit tests
- [x] Added/updated docs and examples
- [x] Ran `make lint` and `make test`
- [x] Added changelog entry

### Screenshots / output
N/A (SDK-only change).

Jordan Lee: For RBAC denied, can we surface the required permission if the server includes it? This will reduce INT-3390 style tickets.

Ethan Park: Pin endpoint supports idempotency keys; can we plumb that through? Also please add a dry-run option so operators can validate without changing state.
Felix Schneider (author): Added `idempotency_key` param and forwarded header. Added `dry_run=True` which sets `validate_only` on the request. Added tests for both.
Chloe Martin: Docs request: please include a snippet that prints the active version and shows how to pin during rollback. Also ensure this doesn’t collide with existing `deployments.pin_*` naming.

Benji Okafor: LGTM after the scope validation tweak.
Ethan Park: Approved.
Jordan Lee: Approved.

source.title: Add route experiment header support + troubleshooting logging
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.release_notes:
- Python SDK: allow passing a stable stickiness key for route experiments and expose assigned experiment/variant headers in responses.
- Add opt-in debug logging to help troubleshoot missing/low-entropy stickiness keys and attribution issues.
source.body: ## Context Redwood Console route-level experiments (A/B/n) uses deterministic assignment based on a stickiness key and returns assignment metadata via response headers. Beta customers and Support reported confusion about how to pass the key and how to confirm which variant a request hit (see SUP-24877, INT-1193). This PR updates the Python SDK to (1) let callers provide a stable stickiness key in a standard way, (2) surface experiment/variant headers on responses, and (3) add opt-in troubleshooting logs that are safe by default. ## What changed - Add `experiment_sticky_key` (and alias `sticky_key`) optional argument to `Client.chat.completions.create(...)`, `Client.responses.create(...)`, and the generic request wrapper. The SDK maps this to request header `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key`. - Parse and expose response headers: `X-Redwood-Experiment-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant`, `X-Redwood-Route-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Config-Version`. These are available via `response.experiment` and `response.headers` (raw) without changing existing payload models. - Add a minimal debug logger gated by `REDWOOD_SDK_DEBUG_EXPERIMENTS=1` (or `client_options.debug_experiments=True`). Logs include which stickiness key source was used (explicit arg vs derived), which headers were emitted, and whether the request used streaming/retry paths. By design, we do NOT log the full stickiness key by default; we log a short hash prefix for correlation and a "low entropy" warning when the key looks accidental (e.g., missing/empty/"unknown"). - Streaming: ensure assignment headers are captured on the initial HTTP response and persisted through stream iteration so callers can still inspect `assigned_variant` even if they only consume tokens. - Retries: make sure the retry wrapper preserves `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key` and does not drop the request-id/experiment headers when constructing the retry request. - Docs/examples: update README snippets to show best practice (use a stable user_id / customer_id, not request_id). Add a short troubleshooting section: "Why do I see skew?" and "Where do I find the assigned variant?". ## Example ```py from redwood import Client client = Client(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"]) resp = client.chat.completions.create( model="redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the following..."}], experiment_sticky_key=user.id, ) print(resp.experiment.experiment_id, resp.experiment.variant_id) # Always available: print(resp.headers.get("x-redwood-experiment-variant")) ``` ## Notes / design decisions - Header naming matches platform ADR/OTel conventions and the Console docs: we normalize access in Python (case-insensitive) but preserve the raw header map. - We intentionally avoid introducing a hard dependency on Console/experiment service types in the SDK; this remains a thin header feature. - Debug logging is opt-in and redaction-first (hash prefix only). ## Testing - Unit tests for header injection + parsing. - Integration-ish tests using `responses` to simulate streaming responses with headers. - Regression test for retry wrapper preserving experiment headers. ## Rollout - Safe to ship as patch/minor; no API removals. - Docs change should reduce Support churn around "missing variant headers" and "how to verify assignment". Related: ENG-9032 docs guide and the examples app update in redwood-examples.
commits: sdk(py): plumb experiment sticky key through request options
sdk(py): parse experiment assignment headers into Response.experiment
sdk(py): preserve assignment metadata for streaming + retries
docs(py): add route experiments troubleshooting + examples
tests: add coverage for header parsing and retry preservation
changed_files: redwood/_streaming.py (capture headers on stream open; persist through iterator)
review_conversation:
Jordan Lee: Can we align the header name with the policy engine? We use `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key` and return `X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant` (string). Also: please confirm we don't ever default to request_id as sticky key.
Dylan Brooks: Updated to `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key` and removed the earlier fallback-to-request-id idea. If callers don't pass a key, we omit the header and emit a debug warning (opt-in).

Logan Wright: For debugging, can we include a correlation-safe identifier? We need to help Support without leaking user identifiers.
Dylan Brooks: Added `sticky_key_hash_prefix` (first 8 chars of sha256) in debug logs only; raw key never logged unless `REDWOOD_SDK_DEBUG_EXPERIMENTS=unsafe` (not documented).

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Any auth/RBAC implications? Make sure we don't expose internal debug endpoints or privileged headers.
Dylan Brooks: No debug endpoints added. Only reads standard assignment headers already returned to customers. No additional scopes.

Caleb Johnson: Streaming header behavior: headers are present only on the initial response, not per chunk. Ensure the SDK stores them before yielding tokens.
Dylan Brooks: Fixed in `Stream.__enter__` to capture headers and attach to the stream response wrapper; added a test that consumes only the iterator and then reads `stream.experiment`.

text: ## Context Redwood Console route-level experiments (A/B/n) uses deterministic assignment based on a stickiness key and returns assignment metadata via response headers. Beta customers and Support reported confusion about how to pass the key and how to confirm which variant a request hit (see SUP-24877, INT-1193). This PR updates the Python SDK to (1) let callers provide a stable stickiness key in a standard way, (2) surface experiment/variant headers on responses, and (3) add opt-in troubleshooting logs that are safe by default. ## What changed - Add `experiment_sticky_key` (and alias `sticky_key`) optional argument to `Client.chat.completions.create(...)`, `Client.responses.create(...)`, and the generic request wrapper. The SDK maps this to request header `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key`. - Parse and expose response headers: `X-Redwood-Experiment-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant`, `X-Redwood-Route-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Config-Version`. These are available via `response.experiment` and `response.headers` (raw) without changing existing payload models. - Add a minimal debug logger gated by `REDWOOD_SDK_DEBUG_EXPERIMENTS=1` (or `client_options.debug_experiments=True`). Logs include which stickiness key source was used (explicit arg vs derived), which headers were emitted, and whether the request used streaming/retry paths. By design, we do NOT log the full stickiness key by default; we log a short hash prefix for correlation and a "low entropy" warning when the key looks accidental (e.g., missing/empty/"unknown"). - Streaming: ensure assignment headers are captured on the initial HTTP response and persisted through stream iteration so callers can still inspect `assigned_variant` even if they only consume tokens. - Retries: make sure the retry wrapper preserves `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key` and does not drop the request-id/experiment headers when constructing the retry request. - Docs/examples: update README snippets to show best practice (use a stable user_id / customer_id, not request_id). Add a short troubleshooting section: "Why do I see skew?" and "Where do I find the assigned variant?". ## Example ```py from redwood import Client client = Client(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"]) resp = client.chat.completions.create( model="redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the following..."}], experiment_sticky_key=user.id, ) print(resp.experiment.experiment_id, resp.experiment.variant_id) # Always available: print(resp.headers.get("x-redwood-experiment-variant")) ``` ## Notes / design decisions - Header naming matches platform ADR/OTel conventions and the Console docs: we normalize access in Python (case-insensitive) but preserve the raw header map. - We intentionally avoid introducing a hard dependency on Console/experiment service types in the SDK; this remains a thin header feature. - Debug logging is opt-in and redaction-first (hash prefix only). ## Testing - Unit tests for header injection + parsing. - Integration-ish tests using `responses` to simulate streaming responses with headers. - Regression test for retry wrapper preserving experiment headers. ## Rollout - Safe to ship as patch/minor; no API removals. - Docs change should reduce Support churn around "missing variant headers" and "how to verify assignment". Related: ENG-9032 docs guide and the examples app update in redwood-examples.

source.title: sdk: raise ResidencyPolicyViolationError for data residency enforcement blocks
source.author: Brian Murphy
source.release_notes: - Python SDK now raises ResidencyPolicyViolationError (subclass of APIError) when the API blocks a request due to region pinning / residency policy. The exception exposes error_code, request_id, and help_url for faster customer troubleshooting.
source.description:
Context: Hosted data residency enforcement returns a deterministic error when a request would violate the org/project/API-key region policy (see ADR-022 + gateway work in ENG-4833). Today the Python SDK surfaces this as a generic APIError, which makes it hard for customers to catch and handle (e.g. prompt the admin to update allowed regions) and for support to quickly pull request traces.

What this PR does: Adds a dedicated exception type, ResidencyPolicyViolationError, and maps residency enforcement responses into it. The exception exposes (a) error_code (machine readable), (b) request_id (for support + audit correlation), and (c) help_url (docs link for remediation). The rest of the response payload is still preserved on the base error for debugging.

Behavior: - If the API returns HTTP 409 with error.code matching residency enforcement (e.g. RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION), raise ResidencyPolicyViolationError. - For all other errors, keep existing behavior (APIError subclasses like RateLimitError, AuthError, etc.). - For streaming: the exception is raised when the initial response is an error; mid-stream transport failures remain unchanged.

Why 409: Aligns with the public error contract for policy blocks (conflict with configured policy) and matches the gateway rollout.

Testing: Added unit tests for mapping and for preserving request_id/help_url. Also added a small integration-style fixture to ensure unknown codes do not get misclassified.

Docs: This PR does not update public docs; it only changes the SDK surface. A separate docs PR will reference the new exception in the Python examples.

Checklist: - [x] Linked to PM-1490 acceptance criteria (machine readable error + actionable guidance) - [x] Added tests - [x] No breaking change (new exception is a subclass of APIError) - [x] Backwards compatible parsing (help_url is optional; request_id may be absent on older gateways)
commits: c4a19d1 sdk/errors: add ResidencyPolicyViolationError class
a1f0b21 sdk/client: map 409 + RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION into dedicated exception
6c9db87 tests: add error mapping tests + fixtures for residency payloads
19a3e6c chore: add typing + docstrings for new error fields
source.changed_areas:
High-level changes:
- redwood_sdk/errors.py: add ResidencyPolicyViolationError(APIError) with fields error_code, request_id, help_url
- redwood_sdk/_response.py (or equivalent): centralized error parsing now detects residency enforcement blocks
- tests/test_errors.py: new tests verifying mapping, field extraction, and fallback behavior

Illustrative payload handled by the parser:
{"error": {"code": "RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION", "message": "Request blocked by data residency policy: allowed_regions=[eu-west-1].", "help_url": "https://docs.redwoodinference.com/data-residency/troubleshooting"}, "request_id": "req_01J..."}

2025-03-04 10:18 Naomi Feldman: Can we ensure the exception name matches product language? We’ve been using “residency policy violation” in the Console copy.
2025-03-04 10:27 Brian Murphy (author): Yep—renamed from RegionPinningError -> ResidencyPolicyViolationError and aligned docstrings with the Console terminology.

2025-03-04 13:02 Marcus Lin: Please confirm we’re not exposing any internal policy identifiers or region routing internals beyond what the gateway returns. Also, make sure request_id is carried through even when error body is missing fields.
2025-03-04 13:16 Brian Murphy (author): Agreed. This maps only code/request_id/help_url + message. No policy_id exposure. Added guard rails so request_id defaults to None but is still available on the base error via headers when present.

2025-03-05 09:41 Wei Chen: Can we keep it as a subclass of APIError so existing try/except blocks still work? And can we add a short note in the changelog?
2025-03-05 09:55 Brian Murphy (author): Yes, subclassing APIError. Added release note entry and docstring showing how to catch specifically.

2025-03-06 11:08 Marcus Lin: LGTM after the request_id fallback change.
2025-03-06 11:21 Naomi Feldman: Approved. Please link this in PM-1490 so we can close acceptance criteria.
2025-03-06 11:33 Brian Murphy (author): Done (linked in Linear + release notes).

source.title: Avoid stripping API keys; only remove trailing CR/LF
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Context: SUP-4821. Some customers store the Redwood API key in secrets managers or copy/paste flows that include a trailing newline. The Python SDK previously used .strip() on the key, which can remove leading/trailing whitespace unexpectedly and cause auth failures when keys are not exactly preserved. Changes: normalize by rstrip("\r\n") only; add a warning if whitespace is detected; add unit tests for newline and carriage-return cases; update docs string in README. Release: v0.9.4.
commits: auth: stop using strip() for api key
tests: add auth key newline fixtures
docs: clarify api_key must be raw key
conversation: Connor OBrien: Does this break anyone who intentionally had spaces?
Ethan Park: We only remove trailing CR/LF now; if someone had leading spaces it would have failed anyway; we emit a warning when whitespace is present.
Aisha Rahman: +1, matches what we saw with Kiteframe. Please add note in release notes.
